topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the faded ink on the first edition of ‘Whispers in the Mire,’ the cover now softened by years of handling. He remembered the breathless excitement of discovering that book, the way the prose had woven a tapestry of sorrow and magic that resonated deep within his own soul. Now, holding it, a cold knot tightened in his chest. He had spent years nurturing that world, building characters in his head, feeling a profound connection to those fictional lives. Then, the revelation hit—the core narrative, the intricate emotional arcs, were lifted, wholesale, from his own unpublished manuscripts. He slammed the book shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet study. His hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room until his breath came in ragged gasps, the air thick and suffocating. Every word he had ever written suddenly felt hollow, a cheap imitation of something genuine he had poured his very essence into.

[story 2]

"It's all just clever maneuvering," Clara muttered, her voice tight, as she stared at the screen displaying the comparison. The article was brutal, dissecting the similarities between her novel, ‘The Obsidian Compass,’ and the celebrated work of Julian Vance. Clara felt a slow, burning heat rise up her neck, a sensation that made her skin prickle uncomfortably. She reread the excerpts, the familiar phrases that had once felt like personal triumphs now tasted like ash in her mouth. A tremor ran through her hands as she gripped the mouse so hard her nails dug into her palms. She pushed back from the desk, leaning against the cool plaster of the wall. Her jaw was clenched so tightly she could feel the strain in her molars. She didn't look at the computer again; instead, she walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, feeling utterly exposed and deeply unsettled.

[story 3]

Marcus carefully placed the annotated manuscript back into its archival box. The silence of the library felt oppressive, amplifying the hollow ache in his stomach. He had always cherished the creative process, the solitary struggle to coax life from blank pages. To discover that someone else had simply mirrored his deepest, most vulnerable creations felt like a profound violation. A deep sigh escaped him, a sound heavy with unspoken frustration. He ran a hand over his face, feeling the rough texture of his stubble, his eyes fixed on a point just beyond the shelf. The injustice of it settled over him like a heavy shroud. He turned, his movements stiff, and walked toward the exit, the weight of the stolen artistry pressing down on his shoulders with crushing force.
